title: Vytynanky: Ukrainian paper cutting
subtitle: Creating the symmetrical cut-paper folk art tradition of Ukraine using folded and single-sheet techniques
excerpt: Vytynanky are Ukrainian cut-paper works with a long folk tradition. They use both folded-and-cut symmetrical forms and single-sheet cutting with scissors or a scalpel to produce lace-like silhouettes of birds, flowers, trees, and geometric borders. The silhouettes are cut from a single colour and read as dark shapes against a contrasting background.
sourceNotes: Ukrainian folk art tradition documented in craft and ethnographic literature. Vytynanky traditions are traced to the 19th century and earlier through ethnographic studies. No single public-domain text covers the home-craft method in full; this entry synthesises documented traditional forms.

glossaryTerms.definition: The plural form of the Ukrainian word for cut-paper art (singular: vytynanka). Vytynanky are traditionally cut from a single black or coloured sheet and mounted against a contrasting surface. They decorate windows, walls, and pottery in the Ukrainian folk tradition.

A papercutting approach in which the design reads as a single-colour silhouette against a contrasting background. All connecting bridges between parts of the design must be maintained during cutting: if a bridge is severed, that part of the design falls away.

A narrow strip of paper connecting two parts of a papercutting design. Bridges maintain the structural integrity of the piece. In lace-like designs, bridges are often as narrow as 1 to 2 mm and must be cut cleanly without being severed.

content.text: belong to a world-wide tradition of cut-paper art. Chinese jianzhi, Polish wycinanki, Mexican papel picado, and German Scherenschnitte are related traditions. Each has its own imagery. The Ukrainian forms use stylised birds, paired figures, trees of life, and geometric borders. The work is a
: one colour against white. The design lives in the shapes removed as much as the shapes kept.

items.name: Light box, A4 (optional, for tracing template)

attrs.heading: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: A scalpel blade is far sharper than a craft knife; treat it accordingly. Change blades when they drag rather than glide. Cut on a self-healing mat. Keep the non-cutting hand entirely clear of the blade's travel path; use a tweezers to hold small cut pieces rather than fingers.

content.text: You also need thin black or coloured paper at 80 to 100 gsm. Thicker paper is hard to cut cleanly in layers. Tissue-thin paper tears. You also need a pencil for drawing the design and white cartridge paper or card for the backing.

The simplest vytynanky are made from a folded sheet. Folding creates instant mirror symmetry, which is characteristic of the tradition. Fold the paper in half, then in half again: four layers give a four-way symmetrical design.

Fold the paper in half lengthwise. Draw your design on the top surface, showing only the shapes to be removed. Leave the fold edge intact, as this connects the two halves. In a simple bird motif, the bird body sits at the fold, wings outward.

Cut out the negative shapes (the parts to remove) through all layers simultaneously, using scissors for curves and a scalpel for any small interior cuts. Interior cuts require a scalpel: pierce the paper at the centre of the shape to be removed and cut outward.

remain connecting all parts of the design. Before opening the fold, run a fingertip across the cut areas to feel for thin strips that need strengthening. A cut that accidentally severs a bridge can sometimes be repaired with a tiny spot of PVA.
Open the fold carefully. The design will be symmetrical along the fold line. Mount on a contrasting background using small spots of PVA at the edges.

The recurring vytynanky images include roosters and doves (representing the sun and the soul), the tree of life with paired birds, sunflowers, geometric flower forms, and running border repeats. Traditional formal work is black on white. Coloured papers are used for seasonal pieces. The proportions are bold. Interior cuts create large open spaces rather than dense all-over lace, which suits the scale of window decoration.
